title: Viswanathan's “Price of Areca Nut” Reply!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below preserves the current OCR transcription as a reading copy; the scanned source remains authoritative. A severely corrupted line in the project-total passage is translated from the readable individual entries and adjacent stated totals, without inventing the damaged characters. The title and closing proverb refer to the Tamil expression about answering a request for directions to Pattukkottai by quoting the price of areca nut: an irrelevant answer that evades the question. The canonical Tamil chapter remains unchanged.
paragraphs: A statement has appeared in a few newspapers under the headline, “Natham Viswanathan Replies to Karunanidhi's Allegation.” In my statement of 9 July 2015, I said: “A retired Electricity Board official has filed a case in the High Court alleging a mega-scam of Rs. 1 lakh crore in the Board, and the High Court has issued notice. The proper course is for the Chief Minister to come forward and explain those allegations before the people's forum.” I was not alone in expressing this view. Leaders of various parties in Tamil Nadu have also demanded an immediate reply from the Chief Minister.

Responding to the Electricity Board mega-scam issue, the Electricity Minister said that the High Court had placed the petition only at the preliminary stage, to determine whether it was fit to be heard. Rather than answer him myself, let me cite the headline under which the English daily *The Times of India* reported the matter on 8 July 2015: “Power Purchase Deals under HC Scanner: Seeks Details of Damning CAG Report after Petition Alleges Rs. 1 Lakh Crore Scam.” Even at a preliminary hearing, it was because of the gravity of the issue and the existence of a prima facie case that the Madras High Court called for the relevant documents for judicial scrutiny.
The newspaper also explained whether the matter was merely at an inconsequential preliminary stage:

> The spectre of an alleged loss of Rs. one lakh crore by the State, due to delayed power projects and purchase of power from private players at exorbitant rates, returned to haunt the Government with the Madras High Court seeking details of a damning Comptroller and Auditor General report on the issue. What was the nature of comments of the CAG? To what extent the explanation was not accepted? What is the view taken by the committee on public undertaking? Firing a volley of such questions at the Government, the first bench comprising Chief Justice Sanjay Kishan Kaul and Justice T. S. Sivagnanam on Tuesday said all relevant materials should be produced before the Court for perusal by August 10.
When the Judges themselves recognised the necessity of firing such sharp questions, the Minister casually dismissed it as only a preliminary hearing.

The Minister also said that C. Selvaraj, the former Electricity Board official who approached the High Court alleging wrongdoing in the Board, had faced disciplinary action and punishment. But Electricity Board Engineers' Association president Gandhi stated on television that Selvaraj appealed against the punishment, was reinstated, and received his back pay.

When I asked the Chief Minister to explain the Madras High Court's severe questions before the people's forum, the Minister twisted it into an allegation that I had issued my statement with a commercial motive. When the time comes, the people's forum will learn in detail who has acted with a dangerous commercial motive in power generation and procurement, and every disguise will fall away!

For one example of the deception practised by the AIADMK regime, consider Jayalalithaa's statement of 8 July 2015: “Through new schemes launched during the past four years and through procurement agreements, a total of 5,346.5 MW of additional electricity has been obtained.” But the Electricity Minister's statement of 11 July 2015 said: “Steps have been taken through long-term and medium-term agreements to make a total of 5,346.5 MW available to Tamil Nadu.” The Chief Minister says 5,346.5 MW “has been obtained.” The Minister says “steps have been taken” to obtain it. Why these differing statements? Who is telling the truth?

The Electricity Minister listed the schemes from which, he said, steps were taken between 2011 and 2015 to obtain this 5,346.5 MW. Let me explain in detail when those schemes were actually begun and under whose governments:

1. **Bhavani Kattalai Barrage II:** begun during the DMK administration in 1996-97 at an estimated Rs. 99.15 crore; 30 MW. 2. **Periyar-Vaigai Hydroelectric Project:** begun during the AIADMK administration in 2004-05 at an estimated Rs. 14.25 crore; 2.5 MW. 3. **Simhadri Stage II, Unit 1:** a Union Government project; 500 MW. 4. **Periyar Hydroelectric Project, Unit III:** begun during the AIADMK administration in 2003-04 at an estimated Rs. 18.83 crore; 4 MW. 5. **Simhadri Stage II, Unit 2:** a Union Government project; 500 MW. 6. **Vallur, Unit 1:** begun jointly by NTPC and the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board during the DMK administration, at an estimated Rs. 8,444 crore; 500 MW. 7. **Periyar Hydroelectric Project, Unit I:** begun during the AIADMK administration in 2004-05 at an estimated Rs. 18.63 crore; 4 MW. 8. **Bhavani Kattalai Barrage III:** begun during the DMK administration in 1996-97 at an estimated Rs. 99.15 crore; 30 MW. 9. **Vallur, Unit 1:** begun during the DMK administration in 2007 at an estimated Rs. 8,444 crore; 500 MW. 10. **Mettur Thermal Power Project, Stage III:** begun during the DMK administration in 2008 at an estimated Rs. 3,550 crore; 600 MW. 11. **North Chennai Thermal Power Station, Stage II, Unit 2:** begun during the DMK administration in 2008 at an estimated Rs. 3,095 crore; 600 MW. 12. **Periyar Hydroelectric Project, Unit IV:** begun during the AIADMK administration in 2004-05 at an estimated Rs. 18.63 crore; 4 MW. 13. **Bhavani Kattalai Barrage III:** begun during the AIADMK administration in 2004-05 at an estimated Rs. 49.40 crore; 10 MW. 14. **Periyar-Vaigai Hydroelectric Project III:** begun during the AIADMK administration in 2003-04 at an estimated Rs. 15 crore; 2.5 MW. 15. **North Chennai, Stage II, Unit 2:** begun during the DMK administration in 2008 at an estimated Rs. 2,718 crore; 600 MW. 16. **Kudankulam, Unit 1:** begun by the Union Nuclear Power Corporation; 1,000 MW. 17. **Vallur, Unit 3:** begun during the DMK administration in 2007 at an estimated Rs. 8,444 crore; 500 MW. 18. **Neyveli Thermal Power Station, Expansion II, Unit 2:** a Union Government project; 250 MW. 19. **Thoothukudi Thermal Power Station, Unit 1:** begun during the DMK administration in 2007 at an estimated Rs. 4,910 crore; 500 MW.

Of these nineteen listed projects, schemes for 3,860 MW had already been undertaken during the DMK administration at an estimated Rs. 22,915 crore. Schemes for only 27 MW had been undertaken during the earlier AIADMK administration at an estimated Rs. 134.47 crore. Projects for 2,250 MW had been initiated by the Union Government, from which Tamil Nadu received 1,459.50 MW.

The Chief Minister and Electricity Minister repeatedly tell the people, as though their own administration had planned this 5,346.5 MW, allocated funds, invited tenders, completed the work and generated the power. Is there any truth in it?

Concealing all these facts, the Chief Minister and Electricity Minister knowingly repeat the falsehood that “5,346.5 MW of additional power has been obtained through new generation schemes planned and launched during the past four years of AIADMK rule.” Whom is this drama intended to deceive?
From the numerous power schemes announced after the AIADMK came to office in 2011, has even one megawatt been produced? What became of those schemes? Why has the Minister not answered?

Instead of replying to Selvaraj's allegation of irregularities in purchasing meters, the Minister said in his statement that the Board's monthly revenue had increased by twenty per cent. Were there irregularities in buying new meters, or were there not? Does the Minister's failure to answer the allegation directly not confirm that irregularities occurred? Moreover, when the Minister himself says the Board's revenue rose by twenty per cent after new meters were installed, is it not true that electricity consumers have been burdened with and made to pay twenty per cent more every month?

An order issued by the Tamil Nadu Electricity Regulatory Commission on 12 September 2014 stated that the Electricity Board could purchase solar power at Rs. 7.01 per unit from producers who established plants and began generation by 12 September 2015. The Commission then extended the order by six months, until 1 April 2016. I asked at the time: why this extension? When solar power was available in other States at Rs. 5.40 per unit, why extend a scheme to buy it at the higher price of Rs. 7.01? Reports said that even the recent memorandum of understanding with the Adani Group fixed the purchase price at Rs. 7.01 per unit. When power was available elsewhere at a lower price, why enter an agreement to buy it at a higher one?

One of the Commission's three members, Nagalsamy, opposed this decision at the time. Was the order nevertheless issued over his objection? When neither the Electricity Board that would buy the power nor the consumers who would use it requested an extension, why did the Commission act on its own to extend the tariff order by six months? Was it for Gujarat's Adani? The extension was not discussed in the Regulatory Commission. It is said that buying electricity at this high contractual price for twenty-five years would cause a loss of Rs. 23,000 crore. What is the Electricity Minister's answer?

Jayalalithaa once blamed the Union Government for the shortage in power generation. But Tamil Nadu Electricity Engineers' Association president Gandhi issued a statement at the time: “Even if generation by central power companies has been affected, are the Tamil Nadu Government's generation projects operating? They are not. In particular, the 600 MW unit at Mettur, North Chennai's second 600 MW unit, and Vallur's first 500 MW unit were completed, fully tested and connected to the grid in March 2012. Yet, for reasons unknown, the Tamil Nadu Government has kept them idle without generating electricity. Because of this, we stand deprived of 1,547 MW.” Why did the Tamil Nadu Government not answer this shocking disclosure?

When inquiries were made in Electricity Board circles, the reply was: “Those projects were introduced during the DMK administration. They are keeping even grid-connected projects from operating precisely so that power may be purchased from private suppliers!”

The Electricity Minister has not answered directly the public's doubts about the Electricity Board mega-scam. Instead, he has tried to divert attention by circling around the issue with statements contrary to fact. Does his reply not recall the proverb: “Ask the way to Pattukkottai, and he tells you that areca nut costs ten paise”?
With affection,
M. K. 14 July 2015
